mission.title: Install and Configure Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK)
mission.description: Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK) automates the deployment and management of the Elastic Stack on Kubernetes. It simplifies the orchestration of Elasticsearch, Kibana, and other Elastic components, making it ideal for users looking to leverage Elastic's capabilities in a Kubernetes environment. Key trade-offs include the complexity of managing custom configurations and the need for persistent storage.

steps.title: Add the Elastic Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the Elastic Helm repository to your local Helm configuration, allowing you to install ECK from the official source.

```bash

helm repo add elastic https://helm.elastic.co

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Elastic Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the ECK operator
steps.description:
This step installs the ECK operator in the 'elastic-system' namespace, which is responsible for managing the Elastic Stack components.

```bash

kubectl create namespace elastic-system

helm install eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.0

```

> Creates a namespace for the ECK operator.
> Installs the ECK operator with a specific version.

steps.title: Verify the ECK operator installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the ECK operator is running correctly in the cluster.

```bash

kubectl get pods --namespace elastic-system

```

> Lists all pods in the elastic-system namespace to verify the operator is running.

steps.title: Deploy an Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step creates an Elasticsearch cluster using a custom resource definition (CRD).

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: elastic-system
spec:
  version: 8.5.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

```

> Creates a basic Elasticsearch cluster with 3 nodes.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: elastic-system
spec:
  version: 8.5.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step checks if the Elasticsearch cluster is running and healthy.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch --namespace elastic-system

kubectl get pods --namespace elastic-system -l app=elasticsearch

```

> Lists all Elasticsearch clusters in the elastic-system namespace.
> Lists all Elasticsearch pods to check their status.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the ECK operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the ECK operator and all associated resources. Be cautious as this may lead to data loss if Elasticsearch clusters are not deleted first.

```bash

helm uninstall eck-operator --namespace elastic-system

kubectl delete namespace elastic-system

```

> Uninstalls the ECK operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the ECK operator
upgrade.description:
This step includes backing up existing configurations, upgrading the operator, and verifying the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch --namespace elastic-system -o yaml > elasticsearch-backup.yaml

helm upgrade eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.1

kubectl get pods --namespace elastic-system

```

> Backs up the current Elasticsearch configuration.
> Upgrades the ECK operator to the specified version.
> Verifies that the ECK operator pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get elasticsearch --namespace elastic-system -o yaml > elasticsearch-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Elasticsearch cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Elasticsearch cluster may not have enough resources or may be misconfigured.

**Fix:**
Check the resource limits and configurations in the Elasticsearch CRD and adjust as necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Elasticsearch'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Elasticsearch service may not be exposed correctly or the pods may not be running.

**Fix:**
Run 'kubectl get pods --namespace elastic-system' to check pod status and 'kubectl describe service my-elasticsearch' to verify service configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient permissions to access the resource'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** RBAC permissions may not be set correctly for the ECK operator.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the necessary roles and role bindings are created for the ECK operator.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create persistent volume claims'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** There may not be enough storage available in the cluster.

**Fix:**
Check the storage class and available storage resources in the cluster.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Elasticsearch 9.x, improved TLS certificate management, and enhanced monitoring capabilities.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of legacy storage classes.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v2.13, ensure that all custom resource definitions are updated to the latest version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Elasticsearch cluster status as 'green' when running 'kubectl get elasticsearch --namespace elastic-system'. The pods should be in 'Running' state.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: elastic-system
spec:
  version: 8.5.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the required resources and the necessary tools installed.
